---
title: "Data Analysis for the Thesis"
author: "Anita Di Gennaro"
date: "2026-04-27"
output: html_document
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

# PHASE 0

This initial phase 0 deals with the data cleaning and preparation of the
real-data backbone of the thesis from two ISTAT sources: SES income data
and RACLI regional data from 2022. The output is three tidy datasets:
*'ses_data', 'racli_clean'*, and *'regional_codes',* that together
describe the structure of Italian wages along the demographic, sectoral,
and geographic macro and micro areas, which will be used as the
experiment's backbone.

In the initial sections, the SES (Structure of Earnings Survey) data was
elaborated and cleaned: a single *parse_ses_table()* function, applied
across the 15 relevant sheets via a metadata table, was used to extract
and label every cell into one long tidy data frame. SES describes wages
by sector, firm size, control type, contract, hours regime, education,
occupation (ISCO), and seniority, but resolves geography only to five
NUTS-1 geographical macro-areas.

Later on, the RACLI regional wage file was filtered to 2022, stripped
from 26 columns to 8 by removing constant and all-NA fields, and recoded
into the same sex and geographic conventions as SES so that the two can
follow a common structure. RACLI describes wages by sex, year, and
quantile (mean, p10, median, p90) across all 20 NUTS-2 regions and 107
NUTS-3 provinces, but does not contain any occupational, sectoral, or
demographic details.

A small bridge table (*regional_codes*) maps each NUTS-2 region to its
NUTS-1 macro-area, accommodating the fact that RACLI still uses the
pre-2021 NUTS coding (ITD, ITE) while the modern standard uses ITH and
ITI. Neither data set alone has the joint distribution of demographics.

```{r results=FALSE}
library(tidyr)
library(readxl)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyverse)
library(stringr)
library(readr)
library(ggplot2)
library(DataExplorer)
```

# SES Wage Structure

This is a series of aggregated tables. In order to understand how to
navigate them, I will start by uploading the excel sheets and looking at
them to then manually work on them based on what I see/need.

```{r}
# this will read the index sheet
# we can see that the first two are indexes - so skip
ses_2022 <- read_excel("data/Tavole_Struttura-delle-retribuzioni_2022.xlsx",
                       skip = 2) # skip blank rows on top


all_sheets <- excel_sheets("data/Tavole_Struttura-delle-retribuzioni_2022.xlsx") # list of all sheets
print(ses_2022)
```

There are 16 tables. Let's understand the layout of one table to see how
to filter that one and move around it.

# Test on Table 1 (tav 1)

```{r}
tav1_raw <- read_excel("data/Tavole_Struttura-delle-retribuzioni_2022.xlsx",
                       sheet = "Tav.1",
                       col_names = FALSE,
                       col_types = "text")   # force everything to text and avoids type-guessing issues



# rename
tav1_col_names <- c( "row_label", "F_educ_low", "F_educ_med", "F_educ_high", "F_total", "sep1",
                     "M_educ_low", "M_educ_med", "M_educ_high", "M_total", "sep2",
                     "T_educ_low", "T_educ_med", "T_educ_high", "T_total")

tav1_raw <- read_excel("data/Tavole_Struttura-delle-retribuzioni_2022.xlsx",
                       sheet = "Tav.1",
                       col_names = tav1_col_names,
                       col_types = "text",
                       skip = 4)
```

## Section types and filter to data rows

The four section markers act as "headers" for the rows below them. The
main idea is to identify them, use fill() to propagate the label
downward, then remove the marker rows themselves and the footnotes.

```{r}
sections <- c("ATTIVITÃ€ ECONOMICA","ATTIVITÃ€ ECONOMICA ",   # ISTAT has a trailing space in some tables so ATTIVITA' ECONOMICA is repeated with a space afterwards too
              "RIPARTIZIONE GEOGRAFICA",
              "CLASSE DIMENSIONALE", 
              "TIPO DI CONTROLLO ECONOMICO")

tav1_clean <- tav1_raw |>
  mutate(
    # when col 1 is NA and col 2 matches a known marker, record the section name
    strat_type = if_else(
      is.na(row_label) & str_trim(F_educ_low) %in% sections,
      str_trim(F_educ_low),
      NA_character_)) |>
  # add section label downward to all rows belonging to that section
  fill(strat_type, .direction = "down") |>
  # drop the section marker rows (row_label is NA) and footnote rows
  filter(!is.na(row_label),
         !str_detect(row_label, "^legenda|^Fonte"),
         !str_detect(row_label, "^None$"))  # in case of leftover "None" strings
  
 
# shorten the section labels to something clean
tav1_clean <- tav1_clean |>
  mutate(strat_type = recode(str_trim(strat_type),
    "ATTIVITÃ€ ECONOMICA" = "sector",
    "ATTIVITÃ€ ECONOMICA "  = "sector",
    "RIPARTIZIONE GEOGRAFICA" = "geography",
    "CLASSE DIMENSIONALE" = "firm_size",
    "TIPO DI CONTROLLO ECONOMICO" = "control_type"))

# rename the levels of education without specifying they refer to education, as I will address it as the table's breakdown category
tav1_clean <- tav1_clean |>
  rename(
    F_low  = F_educ_low,
    F_med  = F_educ_med,
    F_high = F_educ_high,
    M_low  = M_educ_low,
    M_med  = M_educ_med,
    M_high = M_educ_high,
    T_low  = T_educ_low,
    T_med  = T_educ_med,
    T_high = T_educ_high)

tav1_clean
```

## Convert values and drop separator columns

Drop NAs, convert to numeric what is character and turn into NAs "..",
which ISTAT uses to indicate suppressed data (cell too small to
publish). Lastly, the table will be pivoted and the names recoded to be
clearer.

```{r}
tav1_clean <- tav1_clean |>
  select(-c(sep1, sep2)) |>
  mutate(across(.cols = c(F_low:T_total),
                .fns  = ~ as.numeric(if_else(str_trim(.) == "..", NA_character_, .))
                ))

# pivot and mutate
tav1_final <- tav1_clean |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols      = c(F_low:T_total),
    names_to  = c("sex", "breakdown_cat"),
    names_sep = "_",          # split on first underscore 
    values_to = "value") |>
  mutate(sex = recode(sex,
                      "F" = "female",
                      "M" = "male",
                      "T" = "total"),
    breakdown_var = "education",
    outcome_var = "hourly_wage") 
# label what the breakdown variable is and the outcome variable 
  

tav1_final
```

Now the table 1 finalized has the labelled sector as an indicator, the
stratification type, the sex, the reference category (according to which
this table is broken down and categorized - therefore the
breakdown_cat), and the outcome variable, what the values correspond to.

This is a test performed to Table 1 to see if this is a format that
could then be generalized to the rest of the tables, when a complete
data set will be created.

In order for the complete data set to be created, I need to label the
breakdown variables that are used across the tables. SES classifies the
tables according to meta-level variables: education (like tav 1),
contract type, occupation (ISCO), hours regime, seniority brackets.

```{r recoding}
# education (ISCED)
meta_education_level <- list(raw_names = c("row_label","F_low", "F_med", "F_high", 
                                           "F_total", "sep1",
                                           "M_low", "M_med", "M_high", "M_total", "sep2",
                                           "T_low", "T_med", "T_high", "T_total"),
  breakdown_var = "education",
  cat_labels = c(low = "â‰¤ lower secondary",
                    med = "upper secondary",
                    high = "tertiary",
                    total = "total"))
 
# contract type (Tav.2, 7, 12)
meta_contract_type <- list(raw_names = c(
  "row_label",
  "F_permanent", "F_temporary", "F_total", "sep1",
  "M_permanent", "M_temporary", "M_total", "sep2",
  "T_permanent", "T_temporary", "T_total"),
  breakdown_var = "contract_type",
  cat_labels = c(permanent = "permanent", temporary = "temporary", total = "total"))
 
# hours regime (Tav.3, 8, 13)
meta_hours <- list(raw_names = c(
  "row_label",
  "F_parttime", "F_fulltime", "F_total", "sep1",
   "M_parttime", "M_fulltime", "M_total", "sep2",
  "T_parttime", "T_fulltime", "T_total"),
  breakdown_var = "hours_regime",
  cat_labels = c(parttime = "part-time", fulltime = "full-time", total = "total"))
 
# occupation (ISCO) (Tav.4, 9, 15)
# 10 ISCO major groups + total for each sex
# (Forze Armate is almost always ".." but it was included it as its own thing)
meta_ISCO <- list(raw_names = c(
    "row_label",
    "F_armed", "F_manager", "F_intellectual", "F_technical",
    "F_clerical", "F_sales", "F_agriculture", "F_craft",
    "F_operator", "F_elementary", "F_total", "sep1",
    "M_armed", "M_manager", "M_intellectual", "M_technical",
    "M_clerical", "M_sales", "M_agriculture", "M_craft",
    "M_operator", "M_elementary", "M_total", "sep2",
    "T_armed", "T_manager", "T_intellectual", "T_technical",
    "T_clerical", "T_sales", "T_agriculture", "T_craft",
    "T_operator", "T_elementary", "T_total"),
  breakdown_var = "occupation",
  cat_labels  = c(
    armed       = "Armed Forces",
    manager     = "Managers",
    intellectual = "Intellectual professions",
    technical   = "Technical professions",
    clerical    = "Clerks",
    sales       = "Sales & services",
    agriculture = "Agriculture",
    craft       = "Craft & trades",
    operator    = "Machine operators",
    elementary  = "Elementary occupations",
    total       = "total"))
 
# seniority brackets (Tav.5, 10, 16)
# 8 brackets (0-4, 5-9, 10-14, 15-19, 20-24, 25-29, 30-34, 35+) + total
meta_seniority <- list(
  raw_names = c(
    "row_label",
    "F_yr0004", "F_yr0509", "F_yr1014", "F_yr1519",
    "F_yr2024", "F_yr2529", "F_yr3034", "F_yr35plus", "F_total", "sep1",
    "M_yr0004", "M_yr0509", "M_yr1014", "M_yr1519",
    "M_yr2024", "M_yr2529", "M_yr3034", "M_yr35plus", "M_total", "sep2",
    "T_yr0004", "T_yr0509", "T_yr1014", "T_yr1519",
    "T_yr2024", "T_yr2529", "T_yr3034", "T_yr35plus", "T_total"),
  breakdown_var = "tenure",
  cat_labels    = c(
    yr0004 = "0-4 years", yr0509 = "5-9 years",
    yr1014 = "10-14 years", yr1519 = "15-19 years",
    yr2024 = "20-24 years", yr2529 = "25-29 years",
    yr3034 = "30-34 years", yr35plus = "35+ years",
    total = "total"))

# age brackets (table 14) 
# three brackets - 14 to 29, 30 to 49 and 50+
meta_age <- list(
  raw_names = c("row_label",
    "F_young", "F_mid", "F_old", "F_total", "sep1",
    "M_young", "M_mid", "M_old", "M_total", "sep2",
    "T_young", "T_mid", "T_old", "T_total"),
  breakdown_var = "age",
  cat_labels    = c(young = "14-29 years", mid = "30-49 years",
                    old   = "50+ years",   total = "total"))
```

## One generalizing function

-   Input: sheet name, meta information (as in column names + breakdown
    label), outcome label (hourly_wage / annual_wage / annual_hours)

-   Output: tidy long-format tibble

```{r general function}
parse_ses_table <- function(sheet_name, meta, outcome_label) {
  n_target <- length(meta$raw_names)

  raw <- read_excel(
    "data/Tavole_Struttura-delle-retribuzioni_2022.xlsx",
    sheet     = sheet_name,
    col_names = FALSE,
    col_types = "text",
    skip      = 4
  )

  # keep only the columns described by the meta; rename
  raw <- raw[, seq_len(n_target)]
  names(raw) <- meta$raw_names
 
  # detect and propagate section types
  cleaned <- raw |>
    mutate(strat_type = if_else(
      is.na(row_label) & str_trim(.data[[meta$raw_names[2] ]]) %in% sections,
      str_trim(.data[[ meta$raw_names[2] ]]),
        NA_character_)) |>
    fill(strat_type, .direction = "down") |>
    # remove section marker rows, footnotes, and trailing blank rows
    filter(
      !is.na(row_label),
      !str_detect(row_label, fixed("legenda")),
      !str_detect(row_label, fixed("Fonte"))) |>
    # recode section labels
    mutate(strat_type = recode(str_trim(strat_type),
      "ATTIVITÃ€ ECONOMICA"          = "sector",
      "ATTIVITÃ€ ECONOMICA "         = "sector",
      "RIPARTIZIONE GEOGRAFICA"     = "geography",
      "CLASSE DIMENSIONALE"         = "firm_size",
      "TIPO DI CONTROLLO ECONOMICO" = "control_type"))
 
  # drop separator columns
  cleaned <- cleaned |> select(-c(sep1, sep2))
 
  # convert values to numeric and ".." to NA
  value_cols <- setdiff(names(cleaned), c("row_label", "strat_type"))
  cleaned <- cleaned |>
    mutate(across(
      all_of(value_cols),
      ~ as.numeric(if_else(str_trim(.) == "..", NA_character_, .))
    ))
 
  # pivot to long 
  pivot_long <- cleaned |>
    pivot_longer(
      cols = all_of(value_cols),
      names_to  = c("sex", "breakdown_cat"),
      names_sep = "_",
      values_to = "value") |>
    mutate(
      sex = recode(sex, "F" = "Female", "M" = "Male", "T" = "Total"),
      # map short breakdown_cat codes to readable labels
      breakdown_cat_label = recode(breakdown_cat, !!!meta$cat_labels),
      breakdown_var = meta$breakdown_var,
      outcome_var  = outcome_label,
      table_id = sheet_name)
 
  pivot_long
}
```

Test it on table 2.

```{r}
tav2_test <- parse_ses_table(
  sheet_name  = "Tav.2",
  meta = meta_contract_type,
  outcome_label = "hourly_wage"
)
```

Since it worked, let's map all tables to their specific attributes that
are needed in the functions. These information are taken from each
table, that specify the type of outcome and breakdown variable they
refer to.

```{r}
table_map <- tribble(
  ~sheet,    ~meta,             ~outcome,
  "Tav.1",   "education",         "hourly_wage",
  "Tav.2",   "contract_type",     "hourly_wage",
  "Tav.3",   "hours_regime",      "hourly_wage",
  "Tav.4",   "occupation",        "hourly_wage",
  "Tav.5",   "tenure",            "hourly_wage",
  "Tav.6",   "education",         "annual_wage",
  "Tav.7",   "contract_type",     "annual_wage",
  "Tav.8",   "hours_regime",      "annual_wage",
  "Tav.9",   "occupation",        "annual_wage",
  "Tav.10",  "tenure",            "annual_wage",
  "Tav.11",  "education",         "annual_hours",
  "Tav.12",  "contract_type",     "annual_hours",
  "Tav.13",  "hours_regime",      "annual_hours",
  "Tav.14",  "age",               "annual_hours",
  "Tav.15",  "occupation",        "annual_hours",
  "Tav.16",  "tenure",            "annual_hours"
)
 
# named list of all meta vars for lookup
meta_list <- list(
  education = meta_education_level,
  contract_type = meta_contract_type,
  hours_regime = meta_hours,
  occupation   = meta_ISCO,
  tenure = meta_seniority,
  age = meta_age
)
```

# Final SES dataset

Let's try to have the final dataset and see if it works:

```{r}
ses_data <- pmap(
  table_map,
  function(sheet, meta, outcome) {
    parse_ses_table(
      sheet_name = sheet,
      meta = meta_list[[meta]],
      outcome_label = outcome
    )
  }
) |>
  bind_rows()

ses_data |> count(table_id, breakdown_var, outcome_var)
```

What these processes and function gave me:

-   table_id; from which ISTAT source table the row came from

-   strat_type; the primary dimension the table stratifies workers on. 4
    values:

    -   sector: NACE Rev. 2 economic activity

    -   firm_size: number-of-employees bands

    -   geography: Italian macro-region

    -   control_type: public vs. private ownership of companies

-   row_label; the specific category within strat_type (53 unique).
    Concretely:

    -   for *sector*: NACE letter/double-letter codes Bâ€“S with Italian
        descriptions (e.g. "C - AttivitÃ  manifatturiere", "K -
        AttivitÃ  finanziarie e assicurative").

    -   for *geography*: Nord-Ovest, Nord-Est, Centro, Sud, Isole,
        'Totale' (total; maybe to drop?)

    -   for *firm_size*: 10-49, 50-249, 250-499, 500-999, 1000+,
        'Totale' = total (plus two stray Controllo pubblico/privato rows
        that look like leakage from a firm-size Ã— control_type table
        â€” worth a sanity check).

    -   for control_type: public control (controllo pubblico), private
        control (controllo privato), and total (also maybe to be
        dropped).

-   sex; Female, Male, Total.

-   breakdown_var; the secondary dimension cross-tabulated against
    strat_type. 5 'meta' values:t

    -   *tenure*: years with current employer

    -   *occupation*: ISCO-08 1-digit major group

    -   *education:* ISCED level

    -   *contract_type*: permanent vs. temporary

    -   *hours_regime*: full-time vs. part-time

-   breakdown_cat; short machine-friendly code for the secondary
    category (26 unique). Grouped by breakdown_var:

    -   education: low, med, high

    -   contract_type: permanent, temporary

    -   hours_regime: part-time, fullt-ime

    -   tenure: yr0004, yr0509, yr1014, yr1519, yr2024, yr2529, yr3034,
        yr35plus

    -   occupation (ISCO-08 major groups): armed, manager, intellectual,
        technical, clerical, sales, agriculture, craft, operator,
        elementary, total

-   value; the numeric statistic itself. Always interpret in the unit
    implied by outcome_var. No missing values in the file.

'breakdown_cat_label' was developed as a human-readable version of
'breakdown_cat' (e.g. low --\> â‰¤ lower secondary, yr35plus --\> 35+
years, manager --\> Managers etc.), and then kept as the 'breakdown_cat'
variable.

```{r}
ses_data <- ses_data |>
  mutate(breakdown_cat = breakdown_cat_label) |>  
  select(-breakdown_cat_label)

# mutate the regional labeling so that it matches the RACLI organization
ses_data <- ses_data |> mutate(
    nuts1 = case_when(
      strat_type == "geography" & str_detect(row_label, "Nord-Ovest|Nord-ovest") ~ "Nord-Ovest",
      strat_type == "geography" & str_detect(row_label, "Nord-Est|Nord-est")     ~ "Nord-Est",
      strat_type == "geography" & str_detect(row_label, "Centro")                ~ "Centro",
      strat_type == "geography" & str_detect(row_label, "Sud")                   ~ "Sud",
      strat_type == "geography" & str_detect(row_label, "Isole")                 ~ "Isole",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ))


# write_csv(ses_data, file = "ses_data.csv")
```

This data set gives me the full sectorial and demographic structure of
Italian wages in 2022.

# 0.1 Regional-level disaggregation

The final goal is to have these data sets:

-   ses_clean = wages by sector / firm size / control type at NUTS-1 (5
    macro geographical areas)

-   racli_clean = wages by region/province, sex, year quantile at NUTS-2
    (20 regions, 107 provinces) - from which the regional disadvantage
    score can be created. This will data set will not be subject to the
    experiment but will be used to create a score according to which
    MNAR missingness will be engineered.

-   region_keys = combination of NUTS-1 macro-areas and NUTS-2 regions
    (bridge between the sets)

## Adding RACLI regional data

The RACLI data set has four main dimensions: geography (137 territories:
country / NUTS-1 geographical macro areas / 20 NUTS-2 regions / \~107
provinces), sex (Male / Female / Total), year (2014â€“2023, that I
filtered 2022) and wage statistics (mean / p10 / median / p90).

Differently from the SES data, it only has information regarding every
employed person in a region, regardless of education level or
occupation.

```{r}
library(readr)
racli_raw <- read_csv("data/Sesso - prov (IT1,533_957_DF_DCSC_RACLI_8,1.0).csv",
                      quote = "")

racli_raw <- racli_raw |>
  filter(TIME_PERIOD == 2022) # filter 2022
```

Cleaning the variables.

```{r}
racli_clean <- racli_raw |>
  select(geo_code  = REF_AREA,        # ITC, ITC1, ITC11 etc.
         geo_label  = Territorio,      # specific geographical references
         indicator = DATA_TYPE,       # HOUWAG_ENTEMP_AV_MI / FIRD / MED 
         sex_code = SEX,             # 1 males, 2 females, 9 total
         year  = TIME_PERIOD,
         value = Osservazione) |>
  mutate(
    # indicator codes turned into short readable names
    indicator = recode(
      indicator,
      "HOUWAG_ENTEMP_AV_MI" = "mean",
      "HOUWAG_ENTEMP_FIRD_MI" = "p10",
      "HOUWAG_ENTEMP_MED_MI"  = "median",
      "HOUWAG_ENTEMP_NIND_MI" = "p90"
    ),
    # sex codes to match labels in ses_clean
    sex = recode(
      as.character(sex_code),
      "1" = "Male",
      "2" = "Female",
      "9" = "Total"
    ),
    # classify each territory by its NUTS code length:
    #   2 chars (IT)       -> country
    #   3 chars (ITC)      -> NUTS-1 macro-area
    #   4 chars (ITC1)     -> NUTS-2 region
    #   5 chars (ITC11)    -> NUTS-3 province
    geo_level = case_when(
      nchar(geo_code) == 2 ~ "country",
      nchar(geo_code) == 3 ~ "nuts1",
      nchar(geo_code) == 4 ~ "nuts2",
      nchar(geo_code) == 5 ~ "nuts3",
      TRUE                 ~ NA_character_),
    # derive the parent NUTS-1 code for every row (truncate to first 3 chars)
    nuts1_code = if_else(nchar(geo_code) >= 3, substr(geo_code, 1, 3), NA_character_)) |>
  select(geo_code, geo_label, geo_level, nuts1_code, sex, year, indicator, value)

racli_clean
```

## Creating a regional code book

In a specific data set, mostly developed as a 'code book' for reference,
each region will be matched to its macro NUTS-1 area, as well as to its
local code, used in the RACLI data set. It will be a reference for the
codes present both in the RACLI and in the SES data frames.

```{r}
# building directly from the RACLI data so the codes are consistent
regional_codes <- racli_clean |>
  filter(geo_level == "nuts2") |>
  distinct(
    nuts2_code  = geo_code,
    nuts2_label = geo_label,
    nuts1_code) |>
  mutate(
    nuts1_label = recode(
      nuts1_code,
      "ITC" = "Nord-Ovest / North-West",
      "ITD" = "Nord-Est / North-East",   # old NUTS code (used by RACLI)
      "ITH" = "Nord-Est / North-East",   # current NUTS code (post-2021 revision)
      "ITE" = "Centro / Center",     # old NUTS code (used by RACLI)
      "ITI" = "Centro / Center",     # current NUTS code (post-2021 revision)
      "ITF" = "Sud / South",
      "ITG" = "Isole / Islands")) |>
  arrange(nuts1_code, nuts2_code)

# write_csv(regional_codes, file = "regional_codes.csv")
# write_csv(racli_clean, file = "racli_clean.csv")
```

# PHASE 1

## Exploratory Data Analysis - TO EXPAND

The EDA covers the Structure of Earnings dataset before any missingness
is introduced. The goal is to understand their structure, distributions,
and any quality issues, and to produce a first-pass look at the gender
wage gap signal visible in the raw data.

```{r eda-ses-intro}
introduce(ses_data)
plot_intro(ses_data)
plot_missing(ses_data)

# bar plot
plot_bar(ses_data |> select(strat_type, sex, breakdown_var, outcome_var))

# histogram
plot_histogram(ses_data |> select(value))

# boxplot
ses_data |>
  filter(sex %in% c("Female", "Male")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = outcome_var, y = value, fill = sex)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(x = "Outcome variable", y = "Value")
```

```{r eda-ses-corr}
ses_data |>
  filter(strat_type == "geography", sex == "Total",
         row_label != "Totale", breakdown_cat != "total") |>
  select(nuts1, breakdown_var, breakdown_cat, outcome_var, value) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = outcome_var, values_from = value) |>
  select(hourly_wage, annual_wage, annual_hours) |>
  drop_na() |>
  plot_correlation()

ses_data |>
  filter(strat_type == "geography", outcome_var == "hourly_wage",
         sex %in% c("Female", "Male"),
         row_label != "Totale", breakdown_cat != "total") |>
  group_by(sex, breakdown_var) |>
  summarise(
    mean_wage = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd = sd(value, na.rm = TRUE),
    min_val = min(value, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_val = max(value, na.rm = TRUE))
```

-   this data set is much larger and more faceted than the RACLI one,
    and has missing information: NUTS-1 (geographical area, in this case
    would be the country) is NA for non-geography strata rows, but it is
    expected by design. Information regarding armed forces is missing in
    some areas as well.

-   The histogram shows a right-skew of the `value,` because hourly
    wages (\~10â€“20), annual wages (\~15,000â€“25,000), and annual
    hours (\~1,400â€“1,800) are mixed on the same axis. It must be
    differentiated through `outcome_var`. The boxplot of sex x
    outcome_var shows that female earn less than males, for hourly and
    annual wages. The annual hours gap is smaller but still present,
    pointing to both a pay rate and a hours-worked component.

-   The correlation heatmap (hourly/annual wage/annual hours) shows a
    strong relationship between annual wage and annual hours, as could
    be expected. The hourly wage moderately correlated with annual wage,
    while hourly wage nearly uncorrelated with annual hours (possibly
    indicating that full-time hours are compressed regardless of pay).

-   In the summary table (geography rows, hourly wage, sex x
    breakdown_var), occupation shows the highest average wages and the
    widest Female-Male spread. Tenure and education both show clear wage
    gradients, while temporary contract and part-time show the lowest
    values.

# PHASE 2

## 2.1 Measuring gender wage gap

The first phase of this experimental process is to establish a baseline
gender pay gap calculated from real ISTAT 2022 data. A
"composition-adjusted" GPG will be calculated. Using the SES data, for
each NUTS-1 macro area and each breakdown category (education level,
contract type, hours regime, occupation, tenure bracket), compute the
gender gap.

```{r gender-gap-sections}
within_gaps <- ses_data |>
  filter(strat_type == "geography",
         outcome_var == "hourly_wage",
         row_label != "Totale",             # drop the national-total row
         breakdown_cat != "total",    # drop within-breakdown totals
         sex %in% c("Female", "Male")) |>
  select(area = row_label,
         breakdown_var, breakdown_cat,
         sex, value) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = sex, values_from = value) |>
  filter(!is.na(Female), !is.na(Male), Male > 0) |>
  mutate(gap = 1 - Female / Male)

within_gaps
```

aGPG per NUTS-1 area, per breakdown variable. Weight within-category
gaps by the female-wage share of that category within the area, then sum
across categories.

```{r adj-GPG-area-breakdown}
agpg_area_breakdown <- within_gaps |>
  group_by(area, breakdown_var) |>
  mutate(weight = Female / sum(Female, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  summarise(aGPG = sum(weight * gap, na.rm = TRUE),
            n_categories = n(),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(area, breakdown_var)

agpg_area_breakdown

# write_csv(agpg_area_breakdown, file = "agpg_area_breakdown.csv")
```

The composition-adjusted GPG reveals two important patterns. First,
occupation and education are the dimensions where the gap remains
largest and most stable across all macro-areas, confirming that even
within the same job category or education level, men out-earn women.
These are the dimensions where the "unexplained" residual is hardest to
wash away. Second, and most strikingly, the South and the islands flip
negative on `contract_type`, `hours_regime`, and `tenure` (women
out-earn men within full-time/permanent/longer-tenured cells), while
these gaps stay positive in the central regions, and in the North-East
and North-West areas.

A single aGPG number per area, taken as the mean across the five
breakdown variables.

```{r adj-GPG-area}
agpg_area <- agpg_area_breakdown |>
  group_by(area) |>
  summarise(aGPG = mean(aGPG), .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(desc(aGPG))

agpg_area

# write_csv(agpg_area, file = "agpg_area.csv")
```

These tables are the ground-truth baseline against which the imputed
(MICE / LLM) datasets will later be evaluated in Step 4.

!! SELECTION EFFECT - to be looked into for theoretical background !!

## 2.2 Regional disadvantages

A regional disadvantage score (reg_dis) is built from the RACLI 'sex ==
total' cells at NUTS-2 micro level, using two indicators:

-   the first is the regional mean hourly wage, where a low value
    corresponds to a disadvantaged labor market; this is therefore
    reverse-signed and z-standardized, so that higher scores mean lower
    wages.

-   the second is the p90/p10 ratio per region, a standard measure of
    wage dispersion in which a high value signals a more polarized labor
    market (a wider gap between top and bottom earners), and is
    z-standardized in its observed direction.

The two standardized measures are then averaged into a single data frame
'reg_dis' per NUTS-2 region, which is finally categorized into three
labels, low / moderate / high, to give discrete disadvantage levels and
clearer indicators. As a result, this score measures how structurally
disadvantaged a region's labor market is, by combining how low its wages
are with how unequally those wages are distributed. This index will be
used to fabricate MNAR missingness among the data, to further test
recovery methods; MICE with PMM and LLM's reconstruction abilities.

```{r regional-disadv-score}
reg_dis <- racli_clean |>
  filter(geo_level == "nuts2",
         sex == "Total",
         indicator %in% c("mean", "p10", "p90")) |>
  select(geo_code, geo_label, nuts1_code, indicator, value) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = indicator, values_from = value) |>
  mutate(
    p90_p10 = p90 / p10,
    # z-standardize each indicator; low wage and high dispersion = disadvantaged
    z_wage  = -as.numeric(scale(mean)),     # reverse-signed: low wage -> high score
    z_disp  =  as.numeric(scale(p90_p10)),  # "high = bad" direction
    # composite: simple average of the two standardized indicators
    reg_dis = (z_wage + z_disp) / 2,
    # labels
    reg_dis_lvl = cut(reg_dis,
                      breaks = quantile(reg_dis, c(0, 1/3, 2/3, 1)),
                      labels = c("low", "moderate", "high"),
                      include.lowest = TRUE)) |>
  arrange(desc(reg_dis))

# adapted to NUTS-1 for SES
# RACLI codes -> SES macro-area labels, average the composite reg_dis within each macro-area, then re-cut into tiers 
reg_dis <- reg_dis |>
  mutate(
    nuts1 = recode(nuts1_code,
      "ITC" = "Nord-Ovest",
      "ITD" = "Nord-Est", "ITH" = "Nord-Est",
      "ITE" = "Centro",   "ITI" = "Centro",
      "ITF" = "Sud",
      "ITG" = "Isole")) |>
  group_by(nuts1) |>
  summarise(reg_dis = mean(reg_dis), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(reg_dis_lvl = cut(reg_dis,
                      breaks = quantile(reg_dis, c(0, 1/3, 2/3, 1)),
                      labels = c("low", "moderate", "high"),
                      include.lowest = TRUE)) |>
  arrange(desc(reg_dis))

reg_dis 

# write_csv(reg_dis, file = "reg_dis.csv")
```

# PHASE 3

## 3.1 MCAR and MICE pilot

To test the ability and accuracy of LLM's imputation methods, I will
first test the basic MCAR missingness scenario: missing completely at
random.

Under the MCAR assumption, there is no relationship between missingness
and either the wage or the region, as each cell has the same probability
of being missing. This is therefore ideal as a baseline for MAE and
RMSE, it is the baseline 'noise'.

STEPS:

1.  Structure a new data set where about 20% of the SES data,
    specifically on the data relevant for the composition-adjusted GPG
    is missing completely at random (MCAR).

2.  Run MICE with predictive mean matching (PMM) as the standard
    practice. PMM here selected because it is robust, and because it
    draws imputed values from observed values rather than from a fitted
    distribution: every imputed wage is a real euro/hour figure within
    the empirical support.

3.  Pivot back to long, recompute the GPG measure using the already
    present function, as a comparison to understand how well the PMM and
    MICE metrics were at reconstructing the missing data.

```{r}
library(mice)
set.seed(240603) 

# pivoted dataset and filtered to only keep the rows where NUTS1 are specified
ses_wide <- ses_data |>  
  filter(strat_type == "geography",
         outcome_var == "hourly_wage",
         row_label != "Totale",
         breakdown_cat != "total",
         sex %in% c("Female", "Male")) |>
  select(area = row_label,
         breakdown_var, breakdown_cat,
         sex, value) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = sex, values_from = value) |>
  filter(!is.na(Female), !is.na(Male), Male > 0)

# create the baseline corrupted MCAR dataset
miss_rate_ses <- 0.20

ses_missing <- ses_wide |> 
  mutate(
    Female = if_else(runif(n()) < miss_rate_ses, NA_real_, Female),
    Male   = if_else(runif(n()) < miss_rate_ses, NA_real_, Male)
  )

# SAVE ses_wide as ground truth - MNAR missingness will be then applied to it
# write_csv(ses_wide, file = "ses_wide_original.csv")

# SAVE ses_missing as this is MCAR missingness
# write_csv(ses_missing, file = "ses_missing_mcar.csv")
```

### Imputation

MICE imputation using PMM. The SES dataset was filtered to the 5 sheets
reporting hourly wages (Tav. 1-5). Within each sheet, only the geography
stratification rows were kept with the specified 5 NUTS-1 macro-areas
(Nord-Ovest, Nord-Est, Centro, Sud, Isole), and "Total" dropped. Each of
those rows is crossed with the breakdown categories from each table.

```{r}
# convert descriptors to factors so MICE can utilize them as predictors
mice_ses_input <- ses_missing |>
mutate(across(c(area, breakdown_var, breakdown_cat), as.factor)) |>
select(area, breakdown_var, breakdown_cat, Female, Male)

# run the imputation over 5 iterations
mice_ses_fit <- mice(mice_ses_input, m = 5, method = "pmm", printFlag =
FALSE)

# pooling the 5 imputed datasets
imp_female_matrix <- sapply(1:5, function(i) complete(mice_ses_fit,
i)$Female)
imp_male_matrix <- sapply(1:5, function(i) complete(mice_ses_fit,
i)$Male)

ses_imputed_mcar <- ses_missing |>
mutate(
  Female = rowMeans(imp_female_matrix),
  Male = rowMeans(imp_male_matrix))

# write_csv(ses_imputed_mcar, file = "ses_imputed_mcar.csv")
```

Now I have the imputed pilot dataset to establish algorithmic noise for
the data (from which the "composition-adjusted" gpg was measured).
Before evaluating the MICE's performance on MCAR missingness, the entire
missing not at random pipeline will be ran so that the evaluation can be
compared.

## 3.4 Engineered MNAR dataset

Using the regional disaggregation metric that was developed for each
region, the information will be modified in order to fabricate the
missing not at random scenario. This incomplete data will be imputed
both using the standard MICE package, and using the LLMs.

MNAR is the method selected to define missingness because we are
assuming that the probability of the data cells being missing are
directly related to the lack of information/difficulty of collecting
data/increased inequalities in the areas with the lowest possibilities +
lowest female participation. It is a structural issue rather than a
random one, hence the construction of the regional disadvantage levels.
Missingness will be fabricated through MNAR at the macro-area level
according to the (adapted) regional disadvantage index. Only the rows
with the specified stratification type at the geographic level will be
kept (already specified with `ses_wide`).

-   low reg_dis level = \~0.10 probability of mean values being missing

-   medium reg_dis level = \~0.25 probability of mean values being
    missing

-   high reg_dis level = \~0.45 probability of mean values being missing

!! How do I justify/specify this selection of probability scores?

```{r MNAR-ses}
set.seed(240306) # seed for reproducibility

# MNAR: missingness probability rises with disadvantage tier
tier_p <- c(low = 0.10, moderate = 0.25, high = 0.45)

ses_exp_mnar <- ses_wide |>
  rename(nuts1 = area) |>
  filter(!is.na(nuts1)) |>   # keep defined NUTS-1 areas
  left_join(reg_dis |> select(nuts1, reg_dis_lvl), by = "nuts1") |>
  mutate(p_miss = tier_p[as.character(reg_dis_lvl)],             # per-row drop probability
         drop_F = runif(n()) < p_miss, 
         drop_M = runif(n()) < p_miss,  
         Female = if_else(drop_F, NA_real_, Female),
         Male   = if_else(drop_M, NA_real_, Male)) |> 
  select(-drop_F, -drop_M, -p_miss)

plot_missing(ses_exp_mnar)

# sanity check
ses_exp_mnar |>
  pivot_longer(c(Female, Male), names_to = "sex", values_to = "value") |>
  group_by(reg_dis_lvl) |>
  summarise(missing_rate = mean(is.na(value)), n_cells = n(), .groups = "drop")

# SAVE EXPERIMENTAL DATASET
# write_csv(ses_exp_mnar, file = "ses_exp_mnar.csv")
```

Now the two experimental data sets, where missingness was engineered,
are created and can be used to test the two retrieval methods: MICE and
LLM.

# PHASE 4

## 4.1 MICE imputation on MNAR engineered missingness

The standard imputation method using MICE will be tested on the
engineered missingness (not at random) on both data set, to establish
how well this process retrieves the missing data.

The PMM approach is applied to the MNAR-enigneered SES data set. Here
the 20% masking is applied uniformly across the `Female` and `Male` wage
columns of `ses_exp_mnar`: the comparison with the Phase 3 pilot (20%
MCAR) will reveal whether recovery degrades.

```{r mice-mnar-ses}
set.seed(240603)

mice_ses_mnar_input <- ses_exp_mnar |>
  mutate(across(c(nuts1, breakdown_var, breakdown_cat), as.factor)) |>
  select(nuts1, breakdown_var, breakdown_cat, Female, Male)

mice_ses_mnar_fit <- mice(mice_ses_mnar_input, m = 5, method = "pmm", printFlag = FALSE)

# pool: average imputed Female and Male columns
imp_female_mnar <- sapply(1:5, function(i) complete(mice_ses_mnar_fit, i)$Female)
imp_male_mnar   <- sapply(1:5, function(i) complete(mice_ses_mnar_fit, i)$Male)

ses_mice_mnar <- ses_exp_mnar |>
  mutate(
    Female = rowMeans(imp_female_mnar),
    Male   = rowMeans(imp_male_mnar)) |>
  rename(area = nuts1)   # match column name expected by evaluate_imputation

# write_csv(ses_mice_mnar, file = "ses_mice_mnar.csv")
```

This dataset will now be evaluated

## 4.3 Evaluation

One single evaluation function is developed, to be used with different
results throughout the experiment:

```{r eval-ses}
metrics_tracker <- tibble(
  method    = character(),
  metric    = character(),
  n         = integer(),
  mean_bias = double(),
  MAE       = double(),
  RMSE      = double(),
  spearman  = double())

eval_ses <- function(ses_imp, label) {

  # 1. aGPG-level accuracy (25 area x breakdown cells) 
  imp <- ses_imp |>
    group_by(area, breakdown_var) |>
    mutate(gap    = 1 - Female / Male,
           weight = Female / sum(Female, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
    summarise(value_imp = sum(weight * gap, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
    unite("unit", area, breakdown_var, sep = " | ")

  truth <- agpg_area_breakdown |>
    select(area, breakdown_var, value_true = aGPG) |>
    unite("unit", area, breakdown_var, sep = " | ")

  scores <- truth |>
    inner_join(imp, by = "unit") |>
    mutate(bias = value_imp - value_true, abs_error = abs(bias)) |>
    summarise(
      method    = label,
      metric    = "agpg",
      n         = sum(!is.na(bias)),
      mean_bias = mean(bias, na.rm = TRUE),
      MAE       = mean(abs_error, na.rm = TRUE),
      RMSE      = sqrt(mean(bias^2, na.rm = TRUE)),
      spearman  = cor(value_true, value_imp, method = "spearman", use = "complete.obs"))

  metrics_tracker <<- bind_rows(metrics_tracker, scores)
  cat(sprintf("\n--- %s ---\n", label))
  print(scores)

  # 2. Cell-level distributional fidelity
  se <- ses_imp |>
    pivot_longer(c(Female, Male), names_to = "sex", values_to = "value_imp") |>
    left_join(
      ses_wide |> pivot_longer(c(Female, Male), names_to = "sex", values_to = "value_true"),
      by = c("area", "breakdown_var", "breakdown_cat", "sex")) |>
    mutate(bias = value_imp - value_true, abs_error = abs(bias))

  cat("\n=== Cell-level ===\n")
  print(se |> arrange(desc(abs_error)) |>
    select(area, breakdown_var, breakdown_cat, sex, value_true, value_imp, bias))

  ks <- ks.test(se$value_imp, se$value_true)
  cat(sprintf("\nK-S: D=%.4f  p=%.4f\n", ks$statistic, ks$p.value))
  cat(sprintf("Bias direction: %.1f%% of cells over-estimated\n",
              mean(se$bias > 0, na.rm = TRUE) * 100))

  # 3. Fairness by breakdown variable
  cat("\n=== Fairness by breakdown variable ===\n")
  fairness <- se |>
    group_by(breakdown_var) |>
    summarise(bias = mean(bias, na.rm = TRUE),
              MAE  = mean(abs_error, na.rm = TRUE),
              n    = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
    arrange(breakdown_var)
  print(fairness)
  cat(sprintf("MAE ratio (worst / best): %.3f\n", max(fairness$MAE) / min(fairness$MAE)))

  # 4. Fairness by disadvantage tier (MNAR datasets only)
  if ("reg_dis_lvl" %in% names(se)) {
    cat("\n=== Fairness by disadvantage tier ===\n")
    tier_fair <- se |>
      group_by(reg_dis_lvl) |>
      summarise(bias = mean(bias, na.rm = TRUE),
                MAE  = mean(abs_error, na.rm = TRUE),
                n    = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
      arrange(reg_dis_lvl)
    print(tier_fair)
    cat(sprintf("MAE ratio (high / low tier): %.3f\n",
                max(tier_fair$MAE) / min(tier_fair$MAE)))
  }

  # 5. aGPG MAE at two aggregation levels 
  agpg_cells <- ses_imp |>
    group_by(area, breakdown_var) |>
    mutate(gap    = 1 - Female / Male,
           weight = Female / sum(Female, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
    summarise(aGPG_imp = sum(weight * gap, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
    left_join(agpg_area_breakdown |> select(area, breakdown_var, aGPG_true = aGPG),
              by = c("area", "breakdown_var")) |>
    mutate(agpg_error = abs(aGPG_imp - aGPG_true))

  agpg_area_res <- agpg_cells |>
    group_by(area) |>
    summarise(aGPG_imp = mean(aGPG_imp), .groups = "drop") |>
    left_join(agpg_area |> select(area, aGPG_true = aGPG), by = "area") |>
    mutate(agpg_error = abs(aGPG_imp - aGPG_true))

  cat(sprintf("\naGPG MAE (25 cells): %.4f\n", mean(agpg_cells$agpg_error, na.rm = TRUE)))
  cat(sprintf("aGPG MAE (5 NUTS-1): %.4f\n",  mean(agpg_area_res$agpg_error, na.rm = TRUE)))

  if (exists("mcar_mae_ses")) {
    cat(sprintf("MNAR/MCAR MAE ratio: %.2fx\n", scores$MAE / mcar_mae_ses))
  }

  invisible(metrics_tracker)
}

# evaluating MNAR and MCAR MICE retreival
eval_ses(ses_mice_mnar, "MICE_MNAR")
eval_ses(ses_imputed_mcar, "MICE_MCAR")
```

The function produces 5 outputs:

-   **Cell-level SES table**: the same concept but adapted to SES, so
    one row per macro-area x breakdown category x sex cell. It shows
    true vs imputed wage and bias, sorted by largest error as well.

-   **K-S statistic:** it tests whether the imputed wages follow the
    same distribution as the true wages. D close to 0 = distributions
    match well. A low p-value means the imputed distribution is
    significantly different from the true one.

-   **Bias direction:** the percentage of imputed cells where the model
    over-estimated (bias \> 0). It indicates whether the errors are
    systematic or random.

-   **Fairness by disadvantage tier:** the mean bias and MAE broken down
    by low/moderate/high disadvantage regions. If the method performs
    much worse in high-disadvantage areas (which had the most
    missingness engineered into them), the imputation is most likely not
    recovering the structural gap fairly.

EVALUATION OF MICE MNAR RECOVERY: Under MNAR, MICE-PMM underperformes
sharply compared to the MCAR noise floor. The adjusted gap is now
systematically understated (mean_bias = -0.048 across the 25 strata),
MAE rises to 0.107 and RMSE to 0.142, and the Spearman rank correlation
collapses from near-unity to 0.159, meaning the composition-adjusted
regional rankings are effectively not preserved. PMM draws from the
observed information, but when missingness is concentrated in the most
disadvantaged areas, the cells it borrows from are systematically the
wrong ones, so it reconstructs plausible-looking wages that no longer
respect the true ordering between regions.

The K-S statistic is small and non-significant (D = 0.116, p = 0.069),
so the marginal wage distribution is roughly intact. Errors are also
unevenly spread: bias is negative in \~77% of cells (only 22.8%
over-estimated), confirming a one-directional under-recovery, and MAE
varies 3.55x across breakdown variables, worst on occupation (2.011)
where missingness is more dense, and best on tenure (0.566). In short,
MICE is able to reconstruct the wages, but it loses the regional/macro
area structure.

# PHASE 5

# 5.1 Baseline LLM & Uniform Function

Before actually starting the experiment, a baseline analysis of the LLM
will be carried out. Based on previous research, the selected LLM will
be Qwen 3-32B - chosen because the previous model (Olmo) did not have an
available thinking version, and this is a reasoning model. It has a
black box algorithm, differently from the first choice, but the 32B
parameter is a scale that establishes the compression rate, and it is in
line with the Olmo model. (to be expanded with literature)

To actually run it and use it, the weights have to be loaded into the
memory of a machine with a powerful GPU. For computational constraints,
an API key was obtained for 5 dollars (of tokens) from OpenRouter.com.

It will be used through the 'llm_chat' function.

The selected model is the Qwen 3-32B thinking model, and the temperature
and probability were set according to the model's card recommendation.

*temperature* = a scaling factor on the token probabilities before
sampling; "a higher value for temperature produces a softer probability
distribution over classes" (Hinton et al., 2015, pg. 3)
<https://arxiv.org/pdf/1503.02531>

*top_p* = nucleus sampling - a principled alternative to top-k sampling,
which uses the probability distribution to determine a set of tokens to
be sampled from // it selects the highest probability tokens whose
cumulative probability mass exceeds our pre-chosen threshold *p*
[https://arxiv.org/htlm/1904.09751v1](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1904.09751v1){.uri}

In this scenario, with temperature at 0.6 and top_p at 0.9 introduces
controlled stochasticity (as recommended by the model's instructions).
Qwen 3-32B generates with mild randomness rather than deterministically:
the 0.6 temperature keeps the distribution moderately sharp, while top_p
of 0.9 restricts sampling to the most probable tokens covering 90% of
the mass.

This functions turns a prompt into a one single cell number, but because
the parameters that were recommended for the model introduce
stochasticity, the outputs will have to be run N times (starting with
standard 5) to ensure reproducibility. The function has 3 parts:

-   base call for the model, sends the prompt and returns the model's
    raw reply.

-   strip_think + extract_number which extract the numbers from the
    textual answer.

-   impute_cell calls the model N times and takes the mean.

```{r}
library(httr)
library(httr2)
library(jsonlite)

llm_chat <- function(prompt,
                     model = "qwen/qwen3-32b",
                     system = NULL,
                     temperature = 0.6,
                     top_p = 0.9,
                     max_tokens = 8192,
                     retries = 5) {

  msgs <- list()
  if (!is.null(system)) msgs <- c(msgs, list(list(role = "system", content = system)))
  msgs <- c(msgs, list(list(role = "user", content = prompt)))

  for (attempt in seq_len(retries)) {
    resp <- POST(
      url = "https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions",
      add_headers(
        Authorization = paste("Bearer", Sys.getenv("OPENROUTER_API_KEY")),
        `Content-Type` = "application/json"),
      body = toJSON(list(
        model       = model,
        messages    = msgs,
        temperature = temperature,
        top_p       = top_p,
        max_tokens  = max_tokens), auto_unbox = TRUE),
      encode = "raw",
      timeout(300))

    sc <- status_code(resp)

    if (sc == 200)
      return(content(resp, as = "parsed")$choices[[1]]$message$content)

    if (sc %in% c(429L, 503L, 504L) && attempt < retries) {
      message("HF API ", sc, " on attempt ", attempt, " -- retrying in ",
              20 * attempt, "s ...")
      Sys.sleep(20 * attempt)
      next
    }

    stop("API error ", sc, ": ",
         content(resp, as = "text", encoding = "UTF-8"))
  }
  stop("All ", retries, " retries exhausted.")
}

# shared LLM extraction config + helpers (used by EVERY LLM condition)
wage_lower        <- 6       # plausible EUR/hr bounds for RACLI
wage_higher        <- 45

strip_think <- function(x) {
  if (length(x) == 0 || is.na(x)) return(NA_character_)
  x <- gsub("(?s)<think>.*?</think>", "", x, perl = TRUE) # closed trace
  if (grepl("</think>", x)) x <- sub("(?s).*</think>", "", x, perl = TRUE)  # keep text after last </think>
  trimws(x)
}

# primary: first number after stripping the think trace; fallback: LAST number in the raw reply
extract_number <- function(raw) {
  if (length(raw) == 0 || is.na(raw)) return(NA_real_)
  pick <- function(s, last = FALSE) {
    nums <- regmatches(s, gregexpr("-?[0-9]+(?:[.,][0-9]+)?", s, perl = TRUE))[[1]]
    nums <- nums[!grepl("^(19|20)[0-9]{2}$", nums)]   # drop year-like tokens
    if (length(nums) == 0) return(NA_real_)
    as.numeric(gsub(",", ".", if (last) tail(nums, 1) else nums[1]))
  }
  val <- pick(strip_think(raw), last = FALSE)
  if (is.na(val)) val <- pick(raw, last = TRUE)
  val
}

# one call + parse + validate path 
llm_impute_value <- function(prompt, system, sleep = 2,
                             lo = wage_lower, hi = wage_higher,
                             max_tokens = 8192) {
  Sys.sleep(sleep)
  raw <- tryCatch(
    llm_chat(prompt, system = system, max_tokens = max_tokens),
    error = function(e) { message("API error: ", conditionMessage(e)); NULL })
  if (is.null(raw)) return(NA_real_)
  val <- extract_number(raw)
  if (is.na(val))          { message("extract -> NA"); return(NA_real_) }
  if (val < lo | val > hi) { message("out of range: ", val); return(NA_real_) }
  val
}
```

# 5.2 Uninformed baseline on SES

Qwen 3-32B is asked about each missing cells by area and sex only, with
zero data context. This defined the prior knowledge that model carries
about Italian regional wages from training, and the metrics can
therefore be used as a baseline. When using the LLM, the MNAR engineered
missingness will be used, instead of using the MCAR as it was done to
establish the baseline MICE performance and metrics. This is done
because the uninformed LLM ignores all observed data, so the missingness
mechanism doesn't change its guesses or mechanism.

The aim of this phase is to establish Qwen 3-32B's uninformed baseline
on the MNAR-deleted SES cells: for each missing NUTS-1 area x sex wage,
it builds a prompt asking the model for that 2022 mean hourly wage with
no data given, which is then parsed into a single number from the reply.
Furthermore, the function discards implausible values (outside the
established range) as NA, producing one estimated wage per gap. Because
`ses_exp_mnar` has separate `Female` and `Male` columns that both have
NAs, the missing cells are first pivoted to long format to build one
prompt per gap, then the imputed values are pivoted back to wide before
evaluation.

```{r llm-baseline-ses}
set.seed(240603)

sys_ses_base <- "You are an economic estimator. Reply with only a single number."

ses_exp_mnar2 <- ses_exp_mnar |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(Female, Male), names_to = "sex", values_to = "value") |>
  filter(is.na(value)) |>
  select(nuts1, breakdown_var, breakdown_cat, sex)

llm_baseline_ses <- ses_exp_mnar2 |>
  mutate(
    prompt = paste0(
      "What was the mean gross hourly wage for ", sex,
      " workers in ", nuts1, ", Italy, in 2022,",
      " in the ", breakdown_var, " category '", breakdown_cat, "'?",
      " Reply with a single number in EUR per hour, no explanation."),
    value_imp = map_dbl(prompt, \(p) llm_impute_value(p, system = sys_ses_base, hi = 50)))

llm_baseline_ses |> select(nuts1, breakdown_var, breakdown_cat, sex, value_imp)

# write_csv(llm_baseline_ses, file = "llm_baseline_ses.csv")
```

Now it will be merged:

```{r llm-ses}
ses_llm_baseline_merged <- ses_exp_mnar |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(Female, Male), names_to = "sex", values_to = "value") |>
  left_join(
    llm_baseline_ses |> select(nuts1, breakdown_var, breakdown_cat, sex, value_imp),
    by = c("nuts1", "breakdown_var", "breakdown_cat", "sex")
  ) |>
  mutate(value = if_else(is.na(value), value_imp, value)) |>
  select(-value_imp) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = sex, values_from = value) |>
  rename(area = nuts1)

# SAVE
# write_csv(ses_llm_baseline_merged, file = "ses_llm_baseline_merged.csv")
```

The adjusted gap is essentially unbiased (with mean_bias at -0.035),
with a fairly low MAE (0.100) and a moderate ranking correlation
(Spearman 0.532). The cell-level wage inflations seen earlier largely
cancel once collapsed into a composition-adjusted regional gap, which is
probably why the gap-level metrics are not as noisy. The RMSE (0.150),
bigger than the MAE, shows the error remains concentrated in a few
regions.The SES-measured adjusted gap is a smaller,
composition-controlled quantity that's inherently easier to recover.

# PHASE 6 - FIRST ATTEMPTS

Two types of imputation on Qwen 3-32B: free-form contextual prompt and
structured table completion.

## 6.1 Contextual imputation

The model is given one observed row as context and asked to impute the
missing wage for the opposite sex. For SES cells there are no within-row
quantiles, but the counterpart sex's wage sits in the same row, and the
other macro-areas provide cross-area distributional anchors for the same
breakdown category. The context layers are:

-   the counterpart-sex wage in the same area x category cell, if
    observed;

-   the national mean for that sex x category from unmasked reference
    data;

-   the area-level mean for that sex from observed non-missing cells;

-   all other observed areas' wages for the same sex x category, so the
    model can see how the target area relates to the distribution.

```{r ses-ctx-prep}
nat_avg_ses <- ses_wide |>
  pivot_longer(c(Female, Male), names_to = "sex", values_to = "value") |>
  group_by(breakdown_var, breakdown_cat, sex) |>
  summarise(nat_mean = round(mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), 2), .groups = "drop")

area_avg_ses <- ses_exp_mnar |>
  pivot_longer(c(Female, Male), names_to = "sex", values_to = "value") |>
  filter(!is.na(value)) |>
  group_by(nuts1, sex) |>
  summarise(area_mean = round(mean(value), 2), .groups = "drop")

other_areas_ses <- ses_exp_mnar |>
  pivot_longer(c(Female, Male), names_to = "sex", values_to = "value") |>
  filter(!is.na(value)) |>
  group_by(breakdown_var, breakdown_cat, sex) |>
  summarise(
    other_areas = paste0(nuts1, ": ", round(value, 2), " EUR/hr", collapse = "; "),
    .groups = "drop")

ses_obs_long <- ses_exp_mnar |>
  pivot_longer(c(Female, Male), names_to = "sex", values_to = "value") |>
  filter(!is.na(value))

ses_missing_ctx <- ses_exp_mnar |>
  pivot_longer(c(Female, Male), names_to = "sex", values_to = "value") |>
  filter(is.na(value)) |>
  select(nuts1, breakdown_var, breakdown_cat, sex) |>
  mutate(counterpart_sex = if_else(sex == "Female", "Male", "Female")) |>
  left_join(
    ses_obs_long |> rename(counterpart_sex = sex, counterpart_val = value),
    by = c("nuts1", "breakdown_var", "breakdown_cat", "counterpart_sex")) |>
  left_join(nat_avg_ses,     by = c("breakdown_var", "breakdown_cat", "sex")) |>
  left_join(area_avg_ses,    by = c("nuts1", "sex")) |>
  left_join(other_areas_ses, by = c("breakdown_var", "breakdown_cat", "sex"))
```

The imputation:

```{r ses-ctx-impute, cache=FALSE}
sys_ctx <- paste(
  "You have to recover the missing cells in a data set on Italian wages through imputation.",
  "Only use the provided quantile and macro context.",
  "Do not reproduce memorized ISTAT statistics or exact 2022 official wage figures,",
  "strictly basing your outputs only on what is present in the given data.",
  "Be concise in your reasoning. Return one number in EUR/hour, nothing else.")

llm_ctx_ses <- ses_missing_ctx |>
  mutate(
    ctr_line = ifelse(!is.na(counterpart_val),
      paste0("- Observed ", counterpart_sex, " wage (same area and category): ", counterpart_val, " EUR/hr"),
      paste0("- ", counterpart_sex, " wage (same area and category): not observed")),
    prompt = paste0(
      "Macro-area: ", nuts1, " | Sex: ", sex, " ",
      "Category: ", breakdown_var, " - ", breakdown_cat, " ",
      ctr_line, " ",
      "National avg (", sex, ", this category): ", nat_mean, " EUR/hr",
      "Macro-area mean (", sex, ", all categories): ", area_mean, " EUR/hr",
      "Other areas (", sex, ", same category): ", other_areas, " ",
      "Impute: mean gross hourly wage in EUR/hr."),
    value_imp = map_dbl(prompt, \(p) llm_impute_value(p, system = sys_ctx, hi = 50))
  ) |>
  select(-ctr_line)

llm_ctx_ses |> select(nuts1, breakdown_var, breakdown_cat, sex, counterpart_val, value_imp)
# write_csv(llm_ctx_ses, file = "llm_ctx_ses.csv")
```

Merged and evaluation:

```{r ses-ctx-eval}
ses_llm_ctx_merged <- ses_exp_mnar |>
  pivot_longer(c(Female, Male), names_to = "sex", values_to = "value") |>
  left_join(
    llm_ctx_ses |> select(nuts1, breakdown_var, breakdown_cat, sex, value_imp),
    by = c("nuts1", "breakdown_var", "breakdown_cat", "sex")) |>
  mutate(value = if_else(is.na(value), value_imp, value)) |>
  select(-value_imp) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = sex, values_from = value) |>
  rename(area = nuts1)

# write_csv(ses_llm_ctx_merged, "ses_llm_ctx_merged.csv")
```

## 6.2 Structured Table Completion

This prompt will ask the LLM return valid JSON/CSV only, and it will be
given only a small table with missing cells. This last imputation method
is closer to the thesis question, because synthetic data should preserve
relationships across region, sex, and category, not guess cells
independently.

The structured table completion tries to understand whether structured
tabular framing (with an explicit MISSING cell) elicits better
imputation than free-form bullet points, because it more closely
resembles how synthetic data generation actually works. Same context,
same data, same model, just different presentation. Therefore, for the
LLM's imputation we have that:

*H0:* structured tabular framing obtains better imputation results than
contextual imputation (more prompt-structured), because the format is
more similar to actual data generation and formatting.

*H1:* structured tabular framing does not obtain better imputation
results, as it does not differ substantially enough from the contextual
prompt to elicit differences.

```{r tbl-ctx-eval}
# the prompt
sys_tbl <- paste(
  "You are completing a table of Italian gross hourly wages.",
  "Use only the values visible in the table as context.",
  "Do not reproduce memorized ISTAT statistics.",
  "Return only valid JSON with the missing value, nothing else.")

llm_tbl_ses <- ses_missing_ctx |>
  mutate(
    ctr_val = ifelse(!is.na(counterpart_val), paste0(counterpart_val, " EUR/hr"), "MISSING"),
    
      # table-like structure
    prompt = paste0(
      "Area: ", nuts1, " | Sex to impute: ", sex,
      " | Category: ", breakdown_var, " - ", breakdown_cat, "

",
      "| variable | value |
|---|---|
",
      "| ", counterpart_sex, " wage (same area/category) | ", ctr_val, " |",
      "| national mean (", sex, ", this category) | ", nat_mean, " EUR/hr |",
      "| area mean (", sex, ", all categories) | ", area_mean, " EUR/hr |",
      "| other areas (", sex, ", same category) | ", other_areas, " |",
      "| ", sex, " wage | MISSING |",
      'Return {"value": <number>}'),
    value_imp = map_dbl(prompt, \(p) llm_impute_value(p, system = sys_tbl, hi = 50))
  ) |>
  select(-ctr_val)

llm_tbl_ses |> select(nuts1, breakdown_var, breakdown_cat, sex, counterpart_val, value_imp)

# write_csv(llm_tbl_ses, file = "llm_tbl_ses.csv")

ses_llm_tbl_merged <- ses_exp_mnar |>
  pivot_longer(c(Female, Male), names_to = "sex", values_to = "value") |>
  left_join(llm_tbl_ses |> select(nuts1, breakdown_var, breakdown_cat, sex, value_imp),
            by = c("nuts1", "breakdown_var", "breakdown_cat", "sex")) |>
  mutate(value = if_else(is.na(value), value_imp, value)) |>
  select(-value_imp) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = sex, values_from = value) |>
  rename(area = nuts1)

# write_csv(ses_llm_tbl_merged, file = "ses_llm_tbl_merged.csv")
```

## 6.3 Evaluation

Evaluation metrics function, applicable to each LLM's dataset:

```{r eval-ses-llm}
# baseline
eval_ses(ses_llm_baseline_merged, "LLM_uninformed")

# contextual
eval_ses(ses_llm_ctx_merged, "LLM_ctx_v1")

# structured table completion
eval_ses(ses_llm_tbl_merged, "LLM_tbl_v1")
```

The structured table prompt's results were compared to the contextual's
to understand the different performances and to defend the hypothesis.
An initial evaluation comparison through the `eval_ses` function shows
that the differences are very nuanced. No prompt is dominating the
other: contextual is marginally better in the low and high tier (MAE of
0.18 vs 0.20 for structured), while structured is clearly better in the
moderate tier (0.49 vs 0.56). In the high-disadvantage regions the more
telling difference is bias rather than magnitude: structured is
essentially unbiased (0.06), whereas contextual mildly under-estimates
(-0.10). The bias direction shows that both prompts under-estimate wages
overall (mean bias of -0.004 for contextual and -0.006 for structured),
but they behave differently where it matters most. Contextual stays
negative, while structured table completion is essentially unbiased
there and spreads its error more evenly across tiers (a high/low MAE
ratio of 2.33 against 3.0 for contextual).

This is very interesting as the MNAR missingness was engineered to 'hit'
high-disadvantage regions the hardest as a representation of higher
structural inequalities (both social and economic). Structured table
completion is therefore more robust to the regions with the most missing
data, even if it is not strictly more precise overall. Both approaches
fail differently: neither reaches the MCAR noise floor on point
accuracy, yet both preserve the regional ranking far better than MICE
does under MNAR (Spearman ≈ 0.95 against 0.16). The raw per-tier MAE
gaps are small, so no clean "more observed context favors the contextual
prompt" pattern emerges. The most meaningful findings is the
structured's near-zero bias and flatter error profile in the sparsest
regions. Neither hypothesis fully holds: a **partial rejection of
both.** Structured framing matters because it constrains the model's
tendency to under-estimate in data-sparse regions, but it doesn't
strictly perform better or worse than a contextual case.

# PHASE 7

## 7.1 One-shot broad-context imputation

Phase 6 gave the LLM geographic context per cell: the counterpart sex
wage, national mean, area mean, and other regions' values for that exact
category. Phase 7 gives two layers of context at once:

-   The observed MNAR cells (in the target table itself), `ses_exp_mnar`
    all geography × breakdown_var combinations that weren't dropped. The
    LLM still gets geographic anchoring from these, same as Phase 6.
-   The reference table: breakdowns for education, occupation, tenure,
    hours_regime and contract_type (identified in the literature for the
    most influential factors impacting wage inequalities). This adds
    structural depth: the LLM can see how, for example, an occupation
    gap plays out across industries and firm sizes, which informs its
    prediction for an area.

Phase 7 is broader in the sense that it combines geographic anchoring
(from the observed MNAR cells) with structural/demographic depth (from
the reference). The imputation method is also different from Phase 6, as
the LLM here does not 'fill out' the missing cells row by row, but in a
one-shot imputation.

The two data sets used for reference and for the imputation target:

-   **Reference (context)**: `ses_data` rows with
    `strat_type != "geography"`. These cover the same five breakdown
    variables (education, occupation/ISCO, contract type, hours regime,
    seniority/tenure) but cross-tabulated with industry, firm size, and
    ownership type rather than macro areas. Provides a broader context
    to the LLM to predict from.

-   **Target (to impute)**: `ses_exp_mnar`,geography-stratified wages
    with engineered MNAR missingness. Missing cells are marked `MISSING`
    and given an ID. Because the reference and target use different
    stratification axes, there is no overlap: the LLM cannot copy
    answers from the reference.

```{r llm-oneshot-impute, cache=FALSE}
# reference table to give the LLM context: education / occupation / tenure / contract-type
ref_csv <- ses_data |>
  filter(strat_type != "geography",
         outcome_var == "hourly_wage",
         breakdown_var %in% c("education", "occupation", "tenure", 
                              "contract_type", "hours_regime"),
         breakdown_cat != "total",
         sex %in% c("Female", "Male"),
         !is.na(value)) |>
  select(strat_type, strat_value = row_label, breakdown_var, 
         breakdown_cat, sex, value) |>
  mutate(value = round(value, 2),
         row   = paste(strat_type, strat_value, breakdown_var, 
                       breakdown_cat, sex, value, 
                       sep = ",")) |>
  pull(row) |>
  paste(collapse = "\n")

# target: MNAR table -> tag each MISSING cell with a numeric ID so the join is ID-based, to aviod mismatch
mnar_long <- ses_exp_mnar |>
  rename(area = nuts1) |>
  select(area, breakdown_var, breakdown_cat, Female, Male) |>
  pivot_longer(c(Female, Male), names_to = "sex", values_to = "val") |>
  mutate(cell_id = row_number(),
         display = if_else(is.na(val), paste0("MISSING_", cell_id), 
                           as.character(round(val, 2))),
         row = paste(cell_id, area, breakdown_var, breakdown_cat, 
                     sex, display, sep = ","))

mnar_csv  <- paste(mnar_long$row, collapse = "\n")
missing_keys <- mnar_long |> 
  filter(is.na(val)) |> 
  select(cell_id, area, breakdown_var, breakdown_cat, sex)

# prompt
sys_oneshot <- paste(
  "You are completing missing wage cells in an ISTAT Structure of Earnings survey table.",
  "All values are mean gross hourly wages in EUR/hour, Italy 2022.",
  "You are given two tables: a reference table and a target table stratified by NUTS-1 macro-area.",
  "Learn ONLY from the observed reference table, strictly basing your outputs on what is present in the given data.",
  "Each missing cell in the target table is marked MISSING_<id> where <id> is a number.",
  "Return ONLY a two-column CSV with header: cell_id,value",
  "One row per missing cell, where cell_id is the number from MISSING_<id> and value is your imputed wage.",
  "Values must be between 6 and 50. No markdown, no explanation.")

prompt_oneshot <- paste0(
  "REFERENCE TABLE (Italian wage patterns by sector, firm size, and control type)\n",
  "strat_type,strat_value,breakdown_var,breakdown_cat,sex,value\n", ref_csv, "\n\n",
  "TARGET TABLE (geography-stratified wages: impute every MISSING_<id> cell)\n",
  "cell_id,area,breakdown_var,breakdown_cat,sex,value\n", mnar_csv, "\n\n",
  "Return a CSV with header: cell_id,value: one row per MISSING_<id> cell only.")

# imputation
set.seed(240603)
raw_oneshot <- llm_chat(prompt_oneshot, system = sys_oneshot, max_tokens = 32768)

# parse: find the cell_id,value header and read from there
clean_resp <- strip_think(raw_oneshot)
clean_resp <- gsub("```[a-z]*\n?|```", "", clean_resp) # strip markdown fences
lines <- strsplit(trimws(clean_resp), "\n")[[1]]
lines <- lines[nchar(trimws(lines)) > 0]  # drop blank lines

# error catch
if (length(lines) == 0 || all(is.na(lines)))
  stop("LLM returned empty response")

hdr_idx  <- which(grepl("^cell_id,", lines, ignore.case = TRUE))[1]
if (is.na(hdr_idx)) hdr_idx <- 1
csv_text <- paste(lines[hdr_idx:length(lines)], collapse = "\n")

# I() forces read_csv to treat csv_text as literal text, never as a file path
llm_oneshot_df <- read_csv(I(csv_text), show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  mutate(value = suppressWarnings(as.numeric(value)),
         value = if_else(value < wage_lower | value > wage_higher, NA_real_, 
                         value)) |>
  left_join(missing_keys, by = "cell_id") # join by id

# checking the work
returned <- llm_oneshot_df |> filter(!is.na(cell_id), !is.na(value))
cat(sprintf("masked: %d | returned: %d | usable: %d | still NA: %d\n",
            nrow(missing_keys), nrow(llm_oneshot_df),
            nrow(returned), nrow(missing_keys) - nrow(returned)))

print(llm_oneshot_df)

# merged
ses_llm_oneshot_merged <- ses_exp_mnar |>
  pivot_longer(c(Female, Male), names_to = "sex", values_to = "value") |>
  left_join(llm_oneshot_df |> rename(nuts1 = area, value_imp = value) |>
              select(nuts1, breakdown_var, breakdown_cat, sex, value_imp),
            by = c("nuts1", "breakdown_var", "breakdown_cat", "sex")) |>
  mutate(value = if_else(is.na(value), value_imp, value)) |>
  select(-value_imp) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = sex, values_from = value) |>
  rename(area = nuts1)

# write_csv(llm_oneshot_df, file = "llm_oneshot_df.csv")
# write_csv(ses_llm_oneshot_merged, "ses_llm_oneshot_merged.csv")

plot_missing(ses_llm_oneshot_merged)
```

## 7.2 Evaluation

```{r eval-oneshot}
eval_ses(ses_llm_oneshot_merged, "LLM_oneshot_v1")
```

The one-shot approach recovers the composition-adjusted gap very well.
There is a strong regional ranking preservation, the model correctly
orders which areas have larger or smaller gaps. The imputed wage
distribution is statistically indistinguishable from the true one, and
has the best distributional fit of any method tested so far.

Worth flagging:

-   Bias direction: 11.3% over-estimated, 88.7% of cells are
    under-estimated.

-   MAE ratio (tier high/low) = 11.892, the model performs much worse in
    high-disadvantage regions than low ones, even more so than MICE_MNAR
    (4.797). The MNAR design specifically drops more cells in those
    regions, and the broader context doesn't seem to compensate.
